original_abstract: This paper investigates channel estimation within multi-cell, interference-limited cellular networks, focusing on systems ranging from traditional multi-antenna setups to large-scale "massive MIMO" regimes. In these environments, base stations typically mitigate multi-cell interference through localized beamforming; however, the accuracy of these techniques is fundamentally limited by pilot contamination, which remains a primary bottleneck for overall system performance. We propose a novel estimation framework that addresses this challenge by facilitating low-rate coordination between cells during the training phase. Our approach leverages second-order channel statistics to effectively distinguish between interfering users, even when they share identical or strongly correlated pilot sequences. Analytically, we demonstrate that as the number of antennas grows, pilot contamination can be eliminated entirely under specific channel covariance conditions. Numerical simulations confirm that this coordinated strategy yields significant performance gains over conventional estimation methods, even in systems with relatively small antenna arrays.
humanized_abstract: We study channel estimation in the context of multi-cell interference-limited cellular systems. Starting from conventional multi-antenna systems up to massive MIMO scenarios, all common approaches to mitigate multi-cell interference exploit beamforming based on an optimal, interference- cancelling strategy that relies on reasonably good channel estimates. One major obstruction to achieving such high-quality estimates is pilot contamination, which is arguably the biggest impediment to achieving high performance in today’s cellular systems. We propose a new channel estimation technique that coordinates only at low rates during the training stage. Using second-order statistics of the channels, the receivers can exploit the structural properties of the channels between the interfering users, even when the interfering users transmit pilot sequences that are identical or highly correlated to that of the desired user. We show via detailed analysis that high antenna configurations can also lead to a eradication of the pilot contamination effect under certain channel covariance assumptions. Finally, the proposed technique is confirmed by simulations to achieve substantial performance gains compared to the conventional techniques even when employing a modest number of antennas at the BS.
